author: Elvira Clayton
artistdescription: Based in Harlem, Elvira Clayton is a multidisciplinary artist whose work spans installation, performance, sculpture, and oral history. Deeply rooted in historical research, Clayton mines archives and slave-related documents to uncover hidden narratives of American slavery, bringing these forgotten stories to light to honor her own ancestors.  Born in Louisiana and raised in Texas, her diverse practice which includes collage and assemblage serves as a powerful bridge between archival research and contemporary visual storytelling.

projects.title: Shillim Institute/One Landscape Artist Residency : Elvira Clayton.

sections.value: Elvira Clayton’s residency at Shillim unfolded as an intimate journey of listening, immersion, and relational making. Through her visits to nearby villages, she encountered the traditional Maharashtrian textile practice of Godhadi—a deeply meaningful quilt-making form shaped from layered fragments of worn cloth, memory, and domestic care. More than a utilitarian object, Godhadi revealed itself to her as an emotional archive, carrying stories of ancestry, motherhood, ritual, and generational continuity. These encounters opened a profound entry point into the cultural and affective life of the region, where textile became inseparable from lived experience. Moved by the women’s narratives and the embodied knowledge held within these practices, Elvira began to imagine a collaborative process rooted in mutual exchange. This led her to work closely with local women through a series of weaving workshops, where shared making became a space for conversation, trust, and creative reciprocity—honouring both tradition and contemporary artistic dialogue.

Elvira Clayton’s work at the Shillim Art Residency emerges from a nuanced engagement with textile as a site of memory, relation, and cultural transmission. Situated at the intersection of socially engaged practice and material inquiry, her residency unfolded through a sustained attentiveness to the intimate knowledge systems embedded within local craft traditions. What began as an exploration of regional quilting practices gradually evolved into a deeper investigation of textile as a living archive—one through which histories of care, ritual, domestic labour, and intergenerational continuity are held and passed forward. Through visits to nearby villages and conversations with local women, Elvira came to understand textile not merely as material, but as a site where lived experience, memory, and belonging are continually woven together. These exchanges became the conceptual foundation of her residency, prompting a collaborative approach grounded in listening, reciprocity, and shared making. In response to the community’s desire for a practical skill that could be sustained beyond her time at Shillim, Elvira developed a series of weaving workshops using handmade looms constructed from recycled cardboard and simple, accessible materials.Within these gatherings, weaving functioned as both process and metaphor. The repetitive gestures of making created a rhythm of presence and attention, while the workshop space itself became one of dialogue, trust, and mutual learning. As Elvira introduced weaving, she was also invited into the embodied intelligence of local Godhadi practices, allowing vernacular textile knowledge and contemporary artistic inquiry to meet in a meaningful exchange. The participation of children further extended the work’s intergenerational dimension, transforming the workshops into a living continuum of learning and transmission.At Shillim, Elvira’s practice became a meditation on textile as a relational medium—one capable of holding emotion, preserving story, and creating spaces of collective care, where art emerges through connection rather than authorship alone.
